page.title: Your gut is at the centre of everything
page.subtitle: Most people think of the gut as a digestive organ. In reality it's a master communicator — constantly receiving signals from your lifestyle and sending signals back out to your immune system, brain, hormones, heart, and more. Your microbiome report maps this ecosystem.
page.footer: This is why a microbiome report is more than a snapshot of digestion. It maps an ecosystem with connections throughout your body — showing which pathways are working well, which need support, and what can be done to shift them.
page.footer_bold_phrase: ecosystem with connections throughout your body

nodes.label: Diet & Fiber
nodes.desc: What you eat directly feeds or starves your bacteria

nodes.label: Hydration
nodes.desc: Affects gut motility and the microbial environment

nodes.label: Meal Timing & Circadian Rhythm
nodes.desc: When you eat matters; irregular patterns disrupt microbial cycles

nodes.label: Physical Activity
nodes.desc: Exercise increases diversity and butyrate-producing bacteria

nodes.label: Caffeine & Coffee
nodes.desc: Moderate coffee intake linked to greater diversity and more anti-inflammatory bacteria

nodes.label: Stress
nodes.desc: Depletes beneficial bacteria — which then amplifies the stress response

nodes.label: Sleep Quality
nodes.desc: Poor sleep disrupts the gut — and a disrupted gut worsens sleep

nodes.label: Social Connection
nodes.desc: Emerging evidence that social environment influences microbial diversity

nodes.label: Antibiotic Use
nodes.desc: Even a single course can reshape the microbiome for months

nodes.label: Alcohol & Smoking
nodes.desc: Both create a less diverse, less resilient microbial environment

nodes.label: Recreational Drugs
nodes.desc: Opioids, stimulants, and cannabis each produce distinct microbiome signatures with shared declines in SCFA-producing bacteria

nodes.label: Artificial Sweeteners & Additives
nodes.desc: Some sweeteners disrupt microbial balance in a dose-dependent manner

nodes.label: Environmental Exposures
nodes.desc: Toxins and pollutants linked to dysbiosis in recent reviews

nodes.label: Birth Mode & Early Life
nodes.desc: Vaginal birth and breastfeeding are major early microbiome determinants

nodes.label: Age & Life Stage
nodes.desc: The microbiome shifts significantly from infancy through ageing

outputs.label: Immune System
outputs.desc: ~70% of your immune cells live in and around the gut

outputs.label: Brain & Mood
outputs.desc: ~90% of serotonin is made here; vagus nerve carries gut signals to brain

outputs.label: Metabolism & Energy
outputs.desc: Bacteria regulate energy extraction and blood sugar response

outputs.label: Inflammation
outputs.desc: Beneficial bacteria produce SCFAs that keep inflammatory pressure low

outputs.label: Digestive Comfort
outputs.desc: Bloating, regularity, and food tolerance are microbiome-dependent

outputs.label: Cognitive Function & Memory
outputs.desc: Distinct from mood; linked to insulin resistance and neurodegeneration

outputs.label: Heart Health
outputs.desc: Metabolites like TMAO are tied to cardiovascular risk

outputs.label: Weight Regulation
outputs.desc: Influences hunger hormones, fat storage, and caloric extraction

outputs.label: Nutrient Absorption
outputs.desc: Bacteria synthesise vitamins B12, K, and folate and improve uptake

outputs.label: Sleep Architecture
outputs.desc: Microbiome influences serotonin, GABA, and sleep cycle quality

outputs.label: Hormone Regulation
outputs.desc: Influences estrogen metabolism; linked to PCOS and hormonal balance

outputs.label: Skin Health
outputs.desc: Gut–skin axis linked to acne, eczema, and psoriasis

outputs.label: Respiratory & Allergies
outputs.desc: Gut–lung axis affects asthma risk and allergic response
